mcq.q: The layer of gases around Earth is called the…
mcq.explain: The atmosphere contains the air we breathe and protects Earth.

mcq.q: Climate change is caused mainly by…
mcq.explain: Burning fossil fuels releases CO₂, trapping heat.

choices: Heat from the sun near Earth
mcq.explain: Greenhouse gases let sunlight in but trap heat going out.

mcq.q: Which gas is a major greenhouse gas?
mcq.explain: CO₂ is released when fossil fuels burn.

mcq.q: A way YOU can reduce your carbon footprint is…
mcq.explain: Less fuel use = less CO₂.
Warmer oceans strengthen hurricanes; melting ice raises sea levels.

mcq.q: The atmosphere's largest gas by volume is…
mcq.explain: Air is about 78% nitrogen.

mcq.q: The ozone layer protects life by…
mcq.explain: Ozone absorbs ultraviolet radiation from the Sun.

mcq.q: Which is NOT a greenhouse gas?
mcq.explain: Nitrogen makes up most of the air but isn't a greenhouse gas.
CO2 is the main waste gas of burning fossil fuels.

choices: Amount of greenhouse gases caused by your activities
mcq.explain: Your carbon footprint measures climate impact.

mcq.q: Sea level rise is mostly caused by…
mcq.explain: Warmer oceans expand and melted ice adds water.

mcq.q: Which renewable energy is widely used in Jamaica?
mcq.explain: Jamaica is increasing solar generation.

mcq.q: A category 5 hurricane has wind speeds over…
mcq.explain: Cat 5 has winds of 252 km/h or more.

choices: Lose their algae due to stress
mcq.explain: Heat-stressed corals expel their colourful algae and turn white.

mcq.q: Deforestation contributes to climate change because trees…
mcq.explain: Without trees, less CO2 is removed from the air.
Recycling aluminium uses much less energy than making new aluminium.

mcq.q: Which gas keeps Earth warm naturally?
mcq.explain: CO2 traps heat — without some, Earth would be very cold; too much causes warming.
Smog is fog mixed with smoke or other pollutants.

mcq.q: A small action you can take is to…
choices: Walk or cycle for short trips
mcq.explain: Walking or cycling produces zero emissions.

flashcards.back: Total greenhouse gas a person causes

NCDs like diabetes are not passed person-to-person.

Bile from the liver helps digest fats.

Sugar in water — sugar is the solute.

mcq.explain: A hypothesis is an educated guess that can be tested.

mcq.explain: The independent variable is the cause you change.

mcq.explain: The dependent variable is the effect you measure.

mcq.explain: Real scientists record exactly what they observe.

mcq.explain: After analysis, we conclude and communicate findings.

mcq.explain: It's chosen and changed deliberately.

mcq.explain: Constants stop other factors from affecting results.

mcq.explain: Conclusions sum up what was learned.

choices: A result that doesn't fit the pattern
mcq.explain: Anomalies are unexpected results that need investigation.

mcq.explain: The control sees what would happen with no change.

Member of a country with rights and duties
Freedoms and protections every person should have

meta.hook: A story from our world: Living Together

lessons.text: A story from our world: Living Together

mcq.q: Deforestation in Jamaica can lead to…
mcq.explain: Trees anchor soil and absorb water; removing them worsens erosion and floods.
Rainfall feeds rivers and underground aquifers.

mcq.q: A way to reduce plastic pollution is…
mcq.explain: The 3 Rs cut waste at the source.

mcq.q: Mining bauxite helps Jamaica economically but can…
mcq.explain: Mining without rehabilitation harms soils and biodiversity.
Warming and pollution bleach corals.
Use resources so future people can too.

mcq.q: Soil erosion is caused mainly by…
choices: Loss of vegetation and heavy rain
mcq.explain: Without plant cover, water washes soil away.

choices: Water overflows onto normally dry land
mcq.explain: Floods happen when waterways exceed their banks.

choices: Trap sediment and reduce wave energy
mcq.explain: Mangroves are natural shore defenders.

mcq.q: To save energy at home you can…
choices: Switch off appliances when not in use
mcq.explain: Switching off cuts unnecessary use.
Mining can disturb landscapes and pollute water.

mcq.q: A reef damaged by warming oceans undergoes…
mcq.explain: Heat stress causes corals to expel their colourful algae.

choices: Air pollutants reacting with water vapour
mcq.explain: Sulphur dioxide and nitrogen oxides cause acid rain.

mcq.q: To reduce plastic in the sea, communities can…
mcq.explain: Refusing single-use plastics reduces ocean waste.

mcq.q: A wetland is important because it…
mcq.explain: Wetlands clean water and host many species.

choices: Catching at rates fish can recover from
mcq.explain: Sustainable fishing keeps fish populations healthy.
Warming amplifies extreme weather.
Composting recycles food scraps into soil.
Parks safeguard biodiversity.
Reforestation restores forest cover.

meta.hook: A story from our world: Physical Environment & Human Impact

lessons.text: A story from our world: Physical Environment & Human Impact

choices: African, European, Indian, Chinese, Middle Eastern and more
mcq.explain: Jamaica's diversity reflects its history of migration.
We depend on each other for trade, ideas, support.
These are Jamaica's major export earners.
Jamaica imports what it doesn't produce locally.
Culture sharing enriches societies.

mcq.q: An example of Jamaica's global cultural impact is…
mcq.explain: Reggae, jerk, Patois, and sprinters are world-famous.
Globalisation links economies, cultures and people.
Trade involves selling and buying goods/services.
The diaspora are Jamaicans living in other countries.

choices: Money sent home by relatives abroad
mcq.explain: Remittances boost the Jamaican economy.

choices: Sharing of music, food, and ideas
mcq.explain: Exchange enriches all cultures involved.

mcq.q: Jamaica's reggae has spread across the world thanks to artists like…
mcq.explain: Bob Marley made reggae a global genre.
Tourists come from many countries.

mcq.q: Jamaicans share a heritage shaped by…
choices: African, European, Asian and indigenous influences
mcq.explain: Many groups have shaped Jamaican culture.
Multinationals operate across borders.
They open markets between nations.

mcq.q: CARICOM Single Market and Economy supports…
choices: Free movement of goods and people
mcq.explain: CSME helps integration in the region.
The UN helps solve global problems.
Passports identify you when crossing borders.

mcq.q: Climate change is a global issue because…
mcq.explain: Greenhouse gases cross borders, affecting everyone.

flashcards.back: Movement of people to live elsewhere
Shared beliefs, customs, art of a people
Goods sent out to sell abroad
Growing connection of world cultures and economies

meta.hook: A story from our world: Diversity, Interdependence & Culture

lessons.text: A story from our world: Diversity, Interdependence & Culture

mcq.q: The main aim of listening is…
mcq.explain: Listening seeks to understand — not just hear noise.
A strong opener grabs attention.

mcq.q: Formal speech tone is used when…
mcq.explain: Formal = respectful, structured language.
Phones distract — put them away.
Paraphrasing proves understanding.
The same words can mean different things with different tones.
Clarity and eye contact engage listeners.
Active listeners stay engaged.
Pace = how fast you speak.
Persuasion argues for a position.
Informative speeches teach the audience.
Body movements communicate without words.
Pauses give listeners time to process.
Good speakers welcome questions.
Aids should help, not distract.
Constructive feedback guides improvement.

mcq.q: A formal greeting in a speech could be…
mcq.explain: Formal speeches use polite, formal greetings.

mcq.q: In a panel discussion, members should…
mcq.explain: Turn-taking lets ideas be heard.

mcq.q: Rehearsing a speech aloud helps to…
mcq.explain: Practising builds confidence.
Conclusions reinforce the main message.

meta.hook: Words are powerful! Listening & Speaking

lessons.text: Words are powerful! Listening & Speaking

choices: Slowly with pauses at every word
mcq.explain: Fluency = smooth, accurate, expressive reading.
Common words recognised instantly (the, and, was).
Surrounding words give clues to meaning.
Re-, un-, dis- are added to the front.
Prefix 'un-' = not.
Expression conveys meaning to the audience.
Decoding uses phonics to read unfamiliar words.
Expression helps the reader understand meaning.

mcq.q: A suffix is added to the…
mcq.explain: Suffixes go at the end (e.g., -ful, -ness).
Break is the root word.

choices: Words around an unknown word that hint its meaning
mcq.explain: Surrounding words help define unfamiliar terms.
Fluency = pace + accuracy + expression.

choices: Quick reading for the main idea
mcq.explain: Skimming gets the gist.
Scanning hunts for keywords.
Pair and pear sound the same.

choices: A word with the same spelling but different meaning
mcq.explain: E.g., 'lead' (metal) vs 'lead' (guide).
Families share a root, like sing/singer/sang/song.
Practice builds reading skills.

choices: Recognised on sight without sounding out
mcq.explain: Sight words are recognised instantly.
Inflection helps express questions, feelings, etc.

flashcards.back: Nearby words that hint at meaning

meta.hook: Words are powerful! Fluency & Recognition

lessons.text: Words are powerful! Fluency & Recognition

choices: The key message of the text
mcq.explain: Main idea = the core point, supported by details.
You read between the lines using evidence and experience.
Traits are qualities (kind, brave, shy) shown through actions.
PIE — Persuade, Inform, Entertain.
Fiction comes from imagination.
Non-fiction is based on real facts and events.

mcq.q: Theme of a story is the…
mcq.explain: Theme is the central idea.
Protagonists drive the story.

choices: A character who opposes the main character
mcq.explain: Antagonists create conflict.
Setting tells where and when the story happens.
Climax is the most intense moment.

choices: Read between the lines using clues
mcq.explain: Inference fills in what's not directly stated.
Summaries condense main ideas.
Topic sentences orient the reader.

mcq.q: To compare two texts you look at…
mcq.explain: Comparing examines both sides.
Tone is the author's attitude.
Flashback shows past events.

mcq.q: A myth is a traditional story that…
mcq.explain: Myths often explain how things came to be.

choices: A non-fiction book about a real person's life
mcq.explain: Biographies tell real lives.
Examples: buzz, splash.

meta.hook: Words are powerful! Reading Comprehension

lessons.text: Words are powerful! Reading Comprehension

mcq.explain: The subject is who or what performs the action.

choices: Two complete thoughts with a conjunction
mcq.explain: E.g., 'I went home, and I slept.' Two complete ideas joined.
Questions end with a question mark.
Sound alike, different meanings and spellings.
Can't = cannot; apostrophe shows missing letters.
Proper nouns name specific places, people — capitalised.

choices: One independent and one dependent clause
mcq.explain: Complex sentences combine an independent and a dependent clause.
Semicolons join closely related thoughts.
Colons set up lists, examples or quotes.
Words ending in -f often change to -ves.

choices: The cake was eaten by the boy.
mcq.explain: In passive voice, the subject is acted upon.
Active voice — the subject does the action.
Relative pronouns introduce relative clauses.
Greek-origin word; plural is 'criteria'.

mcq.q: Choose the correct verb: 'The teacher, along with the students, ___ in the hall.'
mcq.explain: Subject is 'teacher' (singular) — use 'is'.

choices: She and I went to the market.
Her and I went to the market.
Me and her went to the market.
Her and me went to the market.
mcq.explain: Use 'she and I' as subjects.
'Its' (no apostrophe) shows possession.

mcq.q: Which is the past participle of 'go'?
mcq.explain: Have/has gone — past participle is 'gone'.
'Will' + verb forms simple future.
A sentence needs subject and verb.

meta.hook: Words are powerful! Advanced Grammar

lessons.text: Words are powerful! Advanced Grammar

choices: An original document or firsthand account
mcq.explain: Primary = original (diaries, letters, interviews).
Textbooks and articles that discuss primary sources.

mcq.q: To verify information online, you should…
mcq.explain: Cross-check facts on reliable sites.
Always cite your sources.
Biased news favours one side.

choices: TV, newspapers, internet, radio, social media
mcq.explain: All channels that deliver information.
Primary sources are first-hand.
Secondary sources analyse primaries.
Citations credit the original creators.

choices: Using someone else's work without credit
mcq.explain: Plagiarism is intellectual theft.
Reliable sites verify their info.
Cross-checking reduces errors.
Bias colours how news is presented.
Stereotypes can mislead viewers.
Keywords narrow results.
Bibliographies credit research sources.
It can be edited by anyone, so verify with other sources.
Infographics use images to share data.

mcq.q: A fact differs from an opinion because facts are…
mcq.explain: Facts can be checked.
Peer review boosts reliability.

meta.hook: Words are powerful! Research & Media

lessons.text: Words are powerful! Research & Media

mcq.q: When nervous before a speech, you can…
mcq.explain: Deep breathing and rehearsal calm nerves.
Sleep and review boost performance.

mcq.q: On a multiple-choice test, you should…
mcq.explain: The best answer may be C or D — read them all.
Structured essays are clear and persuasive.

mcq.q: Key words in a question are…
choices: Words that tell you what to do
mcq.explain: 'Compare', 'describe', 'list' tell you the task.

mcq.q: If you finish a test early, you should…
mcq.explain: Review to catch errors.

mcq.q: To prepare for a test, you should…
mcq.explain: Spaced study works best.
Eliminating wrong answers improves your odds.

mcq.q: For an essay, plan with a…
mcq.explain: Outlines keep essays organised.

mcq.q: During a long test you should…
choices: Spend all time on one question
mcq.explain: Time management lets you finish.
Breathing calms the body.
Misreading loses points.
Showing work earns partial credit.
Catching mistakes saves marks.
Mind maps show how ideas connect.
Self-testing strengthens memory.
Spacing helps long-term memory.
Speaking aloud catches stumbles and improves timing.

mcq.q: A 'thesis statement' in an essay is…
mcq.explain: Thesis statements anchor the essay.

mcq.q: On the morning of a test, eat…
mcq.explain: Balanced food helps focus.

meta.hook: Words are powerful! Public Speaking & Test Skills

lessons.text: Words are powerful! Public Speaking & Test Skills

mcq.explain: Brave and courageous have the same meaning.
Opposite of generous is selfish.
From Latin 'bene' = good, 'volens' = willing.

mcq.q: Which best fits: 'The jerk chicken had a ___ aroma.'
mcq.explain: Aromas are captivating, tantalising — teasing the senses.

choices: A phrase with a figurative meaning
mcq.explain: 'Break a leg' = good luck, not literal.
Idiom for saying something ironically or humorously.
Lazy and hardworking are opposites.
Joyful = happy.
Meticulous people pay attention to detail.
Reluctant = hesitant or not wanting to.
Means something easy to do.
To begin friendly talk.
Enormous = huge.
Difficult and challenging mean similar things.
Optimistic people see the bright side.
Shrink = make smaller, opposite of expand.

mcq.q: What does 'novel' mean as an adjective?
mcq.explain: As an adjective, novel = original.
Furious = very angry.
It means start studying hard.
Persevere = persist.

flashcards.back: Word that looks/sounds similar across languages

meta.hook: Words are powerful! Vocabulary Development

lessons.text: Words are powerful! Vocabulary Development

mcq.q: If all Jamaicans love ackee and Kemar is Jamaican, Kemar probably…
mcq.explain: Applying a general rule to a specific case = deduction.
Good arguments use evidence.
Cause → effect. E.g., rain → wet roads.

choices: Jamaica is an island in the Caribbean
mcq.explain: Facts can be verified; the rest are opinions.
Critical readers question and evaluate.

mcq.q: Which is NOT a logical fallacy example?
mcq.explain: Evidence is the opposite of a fallacy.
Conclusions need backing evidence.
Logic links premises to a conclusion.

mcq.q: Cause and effect: rain made the field muddy. Effect is…
mcq.explain: The cause is rain; the effect is the muddy field.

choices: Jamaica is the most beautiful country.
mcq.explain: Facts can be verified.
Critical readers question and evaluate.
Assumptions go without verification.

choices: A leaning that favours one side
mcq.explain: Bias slants the message.

mcq.q: To compare two arguments you look for…
mcq.explain: Comparison weighs both.
Analogies show 'this is like that'.

choices: A broad statement based on examples
mcq.explain: Generalisations cover many cases.

choices: The conclusion is assumed in the premise
mcq.explain: It is a logical fallacy.
Correlation doesn't prove causation.
Critical thinkers analyse and decide better.
Counter-arguments offer the other side.
